title: Quiz: Quantum Errors and Their Nature

questions.question: What does the no-cloning theorem state about quantum information?

options.text: Quantum states can be copied but not deleted
There is no unitary operation that can copy an arbitrary quantum state
Quantum states can only be copied if measured first
Cloning is possible but requires exponential resources

questions.explanation: The no-cloning theorem proves that no unitary U exists such that U|ψ⟩|0⟩ = |ψ⟩|ψ⟩ for all states |ψ⟩. This follows from the linearity of quantum mechanics.

questions.question: How does quantum error correction overcome the no-cloning limitation?

options.text: By using approximate cloning
By entangling the qubit with ancilla qubits instead of copying
By measuring the state before encoding
By limiting to states that can be cloned

questions.explanation: QEC uses entanglement to spread quantum information across multiple qubits without copying. The encoded state |ψ⟩_L is an entangled state of multiple physical qubits.

questions.question: Why can't we simply measure a qubit to check for errors?

options.text: Measurement is too slow
Measurement collapses superpositions, destroying quantum information
Measurement requires too many ancilla qubits
Measurement only works for classical states

questions.explanation: Measuring a qubit in a superposition state collapses it to |0⟩ or |1⟩, destroying the original superposition. The phase information α/β is permanently lost.

questions.question: What is the significance of the Pauli operators {I, X, Y, Z} in quantum error correction?

options.text: They are the only errors that can occur
Any single-qubit error can be decomposed into this basis
They represent measurement operators only
They are used only for phase errors

questions.explanation: Any 2×2 operator can be written as E = e₀I + e₁X + e₂Y + e₃Z. This means if a code can correct each Pauli error separately, it can correct any error that is a superposition of Paulis.

questions.question: How does syndrome measurement handle continuous errors?

options.text: It rejects continuous errors as uncorrectable
It approximates them with the nearest discrete error
It collapses continuous errors into discrete Pauli errors
It ignores the continuous part of the error

questions.explanation: When we measure the syndrome, we project the error onto the Pauli basis. A state like cos(θ)|ψ⟩ + sin(θ)X|ψ⟩ collapses to either |ψ⟩ (no error) or X|ψ⟩ (X error) probabilistically.

questions.question: What type of error has no classical analog?

options.text: X error (bit flip)
Z error (phase flip)
Erasure error
Random bit error

questions.explanation: Phase errors (Z errors) flip the relative phase between |0⟩ and |1⟩ components. Classical bits don't have phase, so this error type is uniquely quantum. It transforms α|0⟩ + β|1⟩ → α|0⟩ - β|1⟩.

questions.question: Syndrome measurement reveals which error occurred without revealing the encoded quantum data.
questions.explanation: True. The key insight of QEC is that syndrome measurement extracts information about the error (which Pauli was applied) without extracting information about the encoded state. This is possible because the syndrome is orthogonal to the code space.
